area: Nutrition data visualization — macro/calorie rings, bars, gauges, and weekly trend charts (2025-2026 best practices)

patterns.name: Single hero calorie ring + macro sub-bars (the 2026 default)
patterns.what: One large primary ring (calories) as the focal element, with the three macros (protein/carbs/fat) shown BELOW as separate horizontal progress bars or a row of three small rings — NOT four concentric nested rings. Hero ring stroke-width ~14-18px on a ~140-180px diameter; rounded line caps (stroke-linecap:round); ~270-360deg sweep. Center holds the load-bearing number.
patterns.why: Concentric multi-rings (Apple-style) distort value perception — outer ring circumference is longer so the same % looks bigger; inner ring needs >1/2 radius to read fairly. Separating calories (ring) from macros (bars) avoids that distortion and gives each macro a linear, comparable scale. MacroFactor and Yazio both lead with one prominent ring; Lifesum uses a single circular score.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor (hero calorie circle), Yazio (single colorful ring + macro bars), Lifesum (circular daily score)

patterns.name: Center content = number-first, label-second
patterns.what: Ring center stacks: BIG primary number (e.g. remaining kcal) in a heavy weight (600-700), a small dimmed unit/label above or below ('kcal', 'remaining'), and optionally a tiny secondary line ('1,840 / 2,200'). Primary number ~32-44px; label ~11-13px uppercase tracked, muted color (~60% opacity). Minimalist: one number dominates, ring is the subtle progress arc around it.
patterns.why: Mobile space is scarce; the user's single most-asked question ('how much do I have left?') must be answerable in one glance. A minimalist large-number-in-ring is the dominant mobile pattern.
patterns.exampleApp: Apple Activity/Fitness, MacroFactor, Yazio

patterns.name: 'Remaining' framing as default, 'consumed' as secondary
patterns.what: Lead with calories REMAINING (goal − consumed) as the big center number when the user is under budget; flip to an over-budget treatment (number turns red/amber, shows '+320 over') when exceeded. Show consumed/goal as a small secondary line. Macros usually shown as consumed-toward-goal (e.g. '120 / 160 g') with the bar filling up.
patterns.why: Remaining is action-oriented ('what can I still eat') and is the framing MyFitnessPal popularized and MacroFactor/Yazio retain. Calories use remaining (budget metaphor); macros use consumed-to-target (achievement metaphor) because protein especially is a floor to HIT, not a ceiling to stay under. Distinct semantics → distinct framing.
patterns.exampleApp: MyFitnessPal, MacroFactor, Yazio

patterns.name: Tabular (monospaced) numerals everywhere numbers update
patterns.what: Apply font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums (font-feature-settings:'tnum' fallback) globally on any element whose digits change — the ring center counter, macro gram readouts, calorie totals, weekly table cells. Pair with a font that ships solid tnum + ideally slashed-zero: Inter, IBM Plex Sans, Roboto, or system-ui/SF.
patterns.why: Proportional digits cause horizontal 'jitter' as a live calorie counter ticks (1 is narrower than 8), and break vertical decimal alignment in weekly tables. tnum forces a fixed-width grid so numbers don't dance. >96% browser support.
patterns.exampleApp: Datawrapper guidance; standard in MacroFactor/Cronometer data tables

patterns.name: Color-coded macros with semantic, consistent hues
patterns.what: Fixed per-macro color used identically across ring, bars, legend, and weekly chart: typically protein=red/pink, carbs=amber/orange, fat=blue/purple (each app picks a palette but locks it app-wide). Calorie ring uses the brand/system accent. Over-target state overrides to red/amber regardless of macro hue. Always ship dark-mode variants.
patterns.why: Color is the only way to map a bar/segment back to its macro without a label; inconsistency between the ring legend and the trend chart forces re-learning. Yazio explicitly 'gamifies intake with colourful rings.'
patterns.exampleApp: Yazio, Lifesum, Cronometer

patterns.name: Progress-ring SVG construction (stroke-dasharray technique)
patterns.what: Each ring/arc = two stacked <circle> elements: a faint full-circle track (the goal, ~10-15% opacity of the fill color) and a foreground arc using stroke-dasharray = circumference and animated stroke-dashoffset to reveal the percentage. stroke-linecap:round for the iconic soft ends; rotate -90deg so fill starts at 12 o'clock. Common stroke-width ~12-32px depending on diameter. Track always visible so the unmet goal stays legible.
patterns.why: This is the canonical, performant, framework-agnostic ring implementation (React+SVG, SwiftUI, plain CSS). The visible track communicates 'how far to goal' even at low completion.
patterns.exampleApp: Apple Activity Rings, react-activity-rings, common CodePen implementations

patterns.name: Weekly trend = bars for discrete totals, area/line for continuous trend
patterns.what: Daily calorie totals over a week → vertical BAR chart (one bar per day, today highlighted, a dashed horizontal goal/target line across). Body-weight or adaptive-TDEE trend → smoothed LINE or soft AREA chart with a trend line through noisy daily dots. Inline sparklines (no axes, ~24-40px tall) for compact per-metric cards. Keep gridlines minimal; label only goal line + min/max.
patterns.why: Bars read as 'separate days you can compare/judge against goal'; a continuous line implies an interpolated trend (correct for weight, wrong for discrete daily intake). MacroFactor's signature is the weight trend line vs noisy scale dots; sparklines give density without chart chrome.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor (weight/TDEE trend line), Cronometer (weekly nutrient bars), MacrosFirst (macro charting)

patterns.name: Over/under-goal state encoding beyond color
patterns.what: Don't rely on red alone for 'over budget' (accessibility). Combine: hue shift (red/amber), an explicit sign ('+320'), word ('over'), and optionally the ring overshooting/doubling its track. Under-goal protein can show an encouraging accent; hitting goal can trigger a subtle fill-complete state.
patterns.why: WCAG — color must not be the only channel. Macro goals have asymmetric meaning (over carbs = caution, over protein = fine) so the visual state must carry direction + magnitude, not just 'bad'.
patterns.exampleApp: MyFitnessPal over-budget red, Apple ring overshoot

animations.name: Ring fill on load / value change
animations.trigger: Dashboard mount or after logging a meal (value updates)
animations.spec: Animate stroke-dashoffset from full (empty) to target offset. ~600-900ms, ease-out (cubic-bezier .22,1,.36,1). On incremental log, animate only the delta. Pair with the center number counting up (tween the displayed integer) using tabular-nums so width stays fixed.
animations.reducedMotion: Honor prefers-reduced-motion: render final ring offset and final number instantly, no count-up, no sweep.

animations.name: Macro bar grow
animations.trigger: Dashboard mount / meal logged
animations.spec: Width/scaleX from 0 to fill%, ~400-600ms ease-out, stagger the 3 macros ~60-80ms each. CSS transform or Framer Motion.
animations.reducedMotion: Snap to final width, no stagger.

animations.name: Over-goal pulse
animations.trigger: Consumed crosses goal threshold
animations.spec: One-shot subtle color cross-fade to red/amber + ~1.05 scale pulse (~250ms) on the affected ring/bar number; do not loop.
animations.reducedMotion: Static color change only, no scale pulse.

accessibility: Color must never be the sole carrier of macro identity or over/under state — always pair hue with a text label, gram value, and sign (+/over). Maintain WCAG AA contrast for the large center number (it's the primary content) and for muted labels against ring backgrounds; muted labels should still clear 4.5:1 or be treated as decorative. Provide aria-label / accessible text on each ring and bar (e.g. 'Protein: 120 of 160 grams, 75 percent'). Ship dark-mode variants of every macro hue. Respect prefers-reduced-motion for all fill/count-up animations. Use tabular-nums so screen-magnifier users don't see reflowing digits.
desktopVsResponsive: Mobile/PWA: single hero calorie ring with number-first center is the focal hero of the screen; macros as a compact 3-up row of small rings or stacked bars below; weekly view is a separate tab/scroll with a 7-bar chart or sparklines (no dense axes). Touch targets >=44px on any tappable ring/segment. Desktop: can afford a wider dashboard — ring on the left with macros AND a weekly trend chart side-by-side, larger axis labels and gridlines, hover tooltips on bars/line points (which don't exist on mobile, so mobile must surface the same values inline as text, not behind hover). Sparklines scale up to full bar/area charts with axes on desktop.

area: Animation & Motion Design for nutrition/health/fitness apps (2025-2026) — implementable with framer-motion 12 (now "motion"), react-circular-progressbar, recharts, canvas-confetti

patterns.name: Two-track motion: spring for physical, ease for visual
patterns.what: Animate physical/spatial properties (x, y, scale, rotate, layout) with springs; animate visual properties (opacity, color, fill) with duration-based ease curves. This is the M3 'spatial vs effects' split and motion 12's default behavior.
patterns.why: Springs on opacity/color produce muddy fades; ease on position feels robotic. Matching property to curve type is the highest-leverage motion decision. M3 codifies it as spatial spring tokens (overshoot allowed) vs effects spring tokens (no overshoot).
patterns.exampleApp: Material Design 3 Expressive, Apple Fitness rings

patterns.name: Ring-fill on data settle, not on mount-spam
patterns.what: Animate calorie/macro rings from previous value to new value with a single eased or low-bounce spring tween (~600-900ms), once per meaningful data change — not on every re-render or tab focus.
patterns.why: Apple Fitness-style rings earn delight by being rare and tied to real progress. Re-animating on every navigation trains users to ignore them. Use react-circular-progressbar AnimatedProgressProvider (valueStart/valueEnd/duration/easingFunction from d3-ease) with pathTransition:'none' to avoid double-animation.
patterns.exampleApp: Apple Fitness, MyFitnessPal, Lifesum

patterns.name: Number count-up synced to the ring
patterns.what: Count the numeric label (e.g. '1,840 kcal') up in lockstep with the ring fill using the same duration and easing, driven by motion useMotionValue + animate() + useTransform rounding.
patterns.why: A static number snapping to final value while the ring sweeps breaks the illusion they are one object. Sharing the motion value (or at minimum identical duration/easing) keeps them coupled.
patterns.exampleApp: Apple Fitness, Cronometer

patterns.name: Confetti reserved for genuine milestones
patterns.what: Fire canvas-confetti only on rare, earned moments (daily goal hit, streak milestone, plan completion) — never on routine logging. Keep particle count <100 for 60fps, set disableForReducedMotion:true.
patterns.why: Library authors stress 'use sparingly — save it for moments that truly deserve celebration.' Confetti on every meal log devalues it; on a 30-day streak it lands. Always respect reduced-motion via the built-in flag.
patterns.exampleApp: Duolingo streak, Strava PR

patterns.name: Meal-complete micro-interaction (check + scale + tint)
patterns.what: On marking a meal/food logged: scale-pop the checkbox (spring, slight overshoot), draw the checkmark, tint the row toward success green — all under ~250ms, ideally via useOptimistic so it fires before server confirm.
patterns.why: Immediate tactile feedback on the most-repeated action. Optimistic + fast spring makes logging feel instant; the green tint is persistent state confirmation without a toast.
patterns.exampleApp: Lifesum, MacroFactor, Apple Reminders

patterns.name: Shimmer/skeleton over spinners for data-bound screens
patterns.what: Use shape-matched skeletons with a left-to-right shimmer sweep (linear, ~1.2-1.5s loop) for dashboard cards, ring placeholders, lists. Pair with the existing loading.tsx streaming skeleton pattern.
patterns.why: Skeletons reduce perceived wait and prevent layout shift versus centered spinners. The shimmer's linear loop is the one acceptable use of linear easing (continuous indicator).
patterns.exampleApp: Facebook, YouTube, most 2025 fitness dashboards

patterns.name: Chart enter + update transitions (recharts)
patterns.what: On first paint animate bars/areas/lines in with recharts isAnimationActive + animationDuration ~400-600ms and slight per-series stagger; on data update let recharts interpolate between datasets rather than remount.
patterns.why: An instant chart reads as static decoration; a 400-600ms eased entrance signals 'this is your data, freshly computed.' Interpolated updates (not remount) preserve continuity when switching week/month ranges.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor weight trend, Whoop

patterns.name: Bottom-sheet with spring + velocity-aware dismiss
patterns.what: Present food-detail / add-entry sheets as draggable bottom sheets that spring up, follow the finger 1:1 while dragging, and dismiss on either a position threshold OR a high downward flick velocity (velocity from drag info). Add a subtle settle bounce at snap.
patterns.why: Velocity-aware dismissal is what makes native sheets feel buttery — a slow short drag snaps back, a fast flick closes even if short. react-modal-sheet (built on motion) implements this; or motion drag + onDragEnd reading info.velocity into animate().
patterns.exampleApp: Apple Maps/Music sheets, react-modal-sheet

patterns.name: List reorder & enter/exit with layout animations
patterns.what: Wrap food log / meal lists so add, remove, reorder animate: motion layout + AnimatePresence, stagger children on initial mount (~30-50ms), add layoutScroll to scrollable ancestors.
patterns.why: When a logged item slides in and pushes neighbors smoothly, the user tracks what changed. Stagger on first load gives a polished cascade; without layoutScroll the offsets are mismeasured inside scroll containers.
patterns.exampleApp: Things 3, Todoist, MacroFactor log

patterns.name: Route/page transitions kept short and directional
patterns.what: Cross-fade or short directional slide (12-20px) between routes at 200-300ms ease-out for enter, ease-in for exit. Avoid full-screen hero animations on frequent navigation; reserve 300-400ms for true modal/detail pushes.
patterns.why: Frequent tab/route changes must feel instant — anything over 300ms on a daily path becomes friction. Directional slide communicates hierarchy (forward/back) without cost.
patterns.exampleApp: Apple HIG navigation push/pop, Linear

patterns.name: prefers-reduced-motion as a first-class branch, not a kill-switch
patterns.what: Detect via useReducedMotion(); when true replace spring/slide/confetti with instant or simple opacity crossfades, keep ring/number as instant value sets, pass disableForReducedMotion to confetti. Keep functional feedback (green tint, check) but drop travel/overshoot.
patterns.why: Accessibility requirement; respects vestibular disorders. Goal is 'remove motion, keep meaning' — the checkmark still appears, it just doesn't bounce. Build the reduced path alongside each animation, not as an afterthought.
patterns.exampleApp: Apple HIG, WCAG 2.3.3

animations.name: Calorie/macro ring fill
animations.trigger: Dashboard data settles (new total after a log, or first load with real data)
animations.spec: duration 700ms (~800-900ms if value jump is large), easing d3-ease easeQuadInOut (cubic-bezier(0.45,0,0.55,1)) OR low-bounce time-spring (bounce 0.1-0.2). Animates SVG stroke-dashoffset (ring sweep) only; color static or crossfades separately. Library: react-circular-progressbar with AnimatedProgressProvider (valueStart=prev, valueEnd=next, duration, easingFunction:easeQuadInOut) + styles pathTransition:'none'. Goal-reached state swaps stroke color via a 200ms color crossfade.
animations.reducedMotion: Set value instantly (valueStart=valueEnd); no sweep. Color/state still reflects goal reached.

animations.name: Number count-up (kcal / grams)
animations.trigger: Same data-settle event as the ring; runs in lockstep
animations.spec: duration + easing IDENTICAL to the ring (700ms, easeQuadInOut). motion: useMotionValue(prev) + animate(mv, next, {duration:0.7, ease:[0.45,0,0.55,1]}) + useTransform(mv, v => Math.round(v).toLocaleString()). Avoid >1s — long counts read as slow. Library: motion (framer-motion 12).
animations.reducedMotion: Render final number directly, no tween.

animations.name: Meal/food logged check-pop
animations.trigger: Tap to mark a meal item complete / log a food
animations.spec: checkbox scale 0.8→1.12→1 spring (stiffness 500, damping 22, mass 0.8) ~220ms; checkmark path draw via pathLength 0→1, 180ms ease-out; row background tint to success green over 200ms ease (color = duration-based, not spring). Fire OPTIMISTICALLY via useOptimistic before server confirm. Library: motion.
animations.reducedMotion: No scale/draw; instantly show checked state + green tint via opacity 0→1 at 120ms (or instant).

animations.name: Milestone confetti burst
animations.trigger: Daily goal achieved / streak milestone / plan completed — RARE only
animations.spec: canvas-confetti: particleCount 80-100, spread 70, originY 0.6, two opposite-angle bursts (55 + 125) for fullness, scalar 0.9, gravity ~1, ticks ~200, startVelocity 35. disableForReducedMotion:true. <100 particles for 60fps. Optional 1-shot scale-bounce on the milestone card behind it (spring stiffness 300 damping 18).
animations.reducedMotion: No confetti (disableForReducedMotion handles it). Replace with static success badge fade-in 200ms + haptic if available.

animations.name: Chart bars/area enter
animations.trigger: Chart mounts or range tab (week/month) changes
animations.spec: recharts isAnimationActive=true, animationDuration 450ms, animationEasing 'ease-out' (cubic-bezier(0,0,0.2,1)); stagger multiple series via animationBegin offsets (0/80/160ms). Bars grow from baseline (scaleY). On data UPDATE keep component mounted so recharts interpolates rather than remounting.
animations.reducedMotion: isAnimationActive=false — bars/areas appear at final size instantly.

animations.name: Bottom-sheet present
animations.trigger: Open food-detail / add-entry / quick-log sheet
animations.spec: enter: translateY 100%→0 spring stiffness 320, damping 34, mass 0.9 (~350-400ms perceived, minimal overshoot). Backdrop opacity 0→1, 200ms ease-out (duration-based). Drag follows finger 1:1. Library: react-modal-sheet (motion-based) or motion drag='y'.
animations.reducedMotion: translateY replaced by opacity 0→1 fade 200ms; no drag-spring, dismiss is plain fade-out.

animations.name: Bottom-sheet velocity dismiss
animations.trigger: Drag-release on an open sheet
animations.spec: onDragEnd: if offset.y > 0.4*height OR info.velocity.y > 800 px/s → animate to closed with type:'inertia'/spring seeded by velocity (motion carries gesture velocity into the spring); else spring back to snap (stiffness 500, damping 40, slight settle). Threshold uses BOTH position and velocity so a fast short flick closes.
animations.reducedMotion: Threshold crossed → instant/200ms fade close; no inertial throw.

animations.name: List item add / remove
animations.trigger: New food logged (insert) or item deleted
animations.spec: AnimatePresence: enter initial {opacity:0, height:0, y:-8} → {opacity:1, height:'auto', y:0} spring stiffness 400 damping 30; exit {opacity:0, height:0} 200ms ease-in. Neighbors reflow via layout prop. Add layoutScroll to the scroll container so offsets measure correctly. Library: motion.
animations.reducedMotion: opacity-only enter/exit 150ms; no height/y travel (set height instantly to avoid context-free layout jank).

animations.name: List initial stagger cascade
animations.trigger: Log/list first mounts with items
animations.spec: parent variants staggerChildren 0.035, delayChildren 0.05; child {opacity:0,y:12}→{opacity:1,y:0} spring stiffness 500 damping 32 (~250ms each). For long lists clamp stagger so last item starts <400ms in. Library: motion.
animations.reducedMotion: Disable stagger (staggerChildren:0); all items opacity 0→1 together at 150ms or instant.

animations.name: Route transition
animations.trigger: Navigate between app routes/tabs
animations.spec: enter: opacity 0→1 + y 12→0, 240ms ease-out cubic-bezier(0,0,0.2,1); exit: opacity 1→0, 180ms ease-in cubic-bezier(0.4,0,1,1). Detail/modal push may use 320-380ms slide-from-right. Keep frequent paths ≤300ms. Library: motion AnimatePresence mode='wait' or 'popLayout'.
animations.reducedMotion: Crossfade only, opacity 0→1 at 150ms, no y travel, no directional slide.

animations.name: Skeleton shimmer
animations.trigger: Data-bound screen loading (dashboard cards, ring placeholder, list)
animations.spec: linear-gradient highlight sweeping left→right, transform translateX(-100%→100%), duration 1.3s, ease linear, infinite loop. Shape-matched to final content to avoid layout shift. The ONLY acceptable linear easing (continuous indicator).
animations.reducedMotion: Static muted placeholder block, no sweep (or a slow ≤0.8Hz opacity pulse 0.5↔1).

animations.name: Button/toggle micro-tap
animations.trigger: Any primary button / toggle / segmented control tap
animations.spec: whileTap scale 0.96, spring stiffness 600 damping 30 (~120ms); toggle thumb travel spring stiffness 700 damping 35. Keep micro-interactions 80-150ms — they fire constantly so must feel instant. Library: motion whileTap.
animations.reducedMotion: Drop scale; rely on color/opacity state change only, instant or ≤100ms.

desktopVsResponsive: Bottom sheets are mobile/PWA-only: on desktop the same content becomes a centered modal or right-side panel (scale 0.96->1 + opacity, ~200ms ease-out) with NO drag-dismiss — there is no touch flick gesture, so the velocity logic is dropped and dismiss is click-backdrop/Esc. Touch gestures (drag handles, swipe-to-delete on log rows) exist only at the touch breakpoint; on desktop expose explicit buttons. Springs can be marginally snappier/lower-bounce on desktop where pointer precision is higher; mobile benefits from slightly more travel for legibility on small screens. Confetti origin/spread should account for viewport — mobile fire from originY ~0.6 (above the thumb), desktop center. Respect the project mobile-viewport rules: drive any full-height motion containers with h-dvh, never h-screen/100vh outside md:. Gate gesture-heavy interactions on both reduced-motion AND coarse-pointer detection.
accessibility: prefers-reduced-motion is mandatory and must be a real branch, not a global disable: every animation ships a reduced variant that preserves MEANING (the checkmark, green tint, final ring value, success badge) while removing TRAVEL and OVERSHOOT (no slide, no bounce, no confetti, no count-up sweep). Use motion useReducedMotion() to switch variants and pass disableForReducedMotion:true to canvas-confetti (it reads the OS setting). Never convey state through motion alone — pair every animated state change with a static cue (color, icon, text) so it survives reduced-motion. Keep contrast on success-green tints at WCAG AA against row backgrounds in both light and dark themes (project requires dark-mode variants on all new components). Avoid large-area flashing/strobe (confetti density capped, no >3 flashes/sec) per WCAG 2.3.1. Animated content must not block interaction: optimistic check-pop must not delay the actual tap handler. Skeleton shimmer reduces to a static or slow <=0.8Hz pulse under reduced-motion. When in doubt, the functional instant path is the safe default.

area: Food-logging UX micro-interactions WITHOUT camera/AI — fast manual entry (quick-add, recents, favorites, copy-meal, swipe/tap-to-log, portion steppers, search-as-you-type, bottom-sheet flow, optimistic+undo, haptics, empty states). 2025-2026 patterns from MacroFactor, MyFitnessPal, Cronometer.

patterns.name: Pre-search surfaced lists (favorites + recents + hourly go-tos)
patterns.what: Before the user types a single character, the search screen renders three stacked sources: horizontally-scrollable Favorites pinned at the very top, then 'hourly go-tos' (foods frequently logged at/near the current hour, ranked by recency+frequency), then a vertical 'Latest/Recents' list. Typing collapses these into ranked result groups (From History / Custom / Common / Branded).
patterns.why: The single biggest friction killer: most logs are repeats. Surfacing the likely answer with zero keystrokes turns a multi-step search into a one-tap action. Time-of-day weighting means coffee+oatmeal appear together at 9am without searching.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor (Favorites top, hourly go-tos, Latest), MyFitnessPal (recents tab), Cronometer (saved favorites one-tap)

patterns.name: Multi-add without leaving search (plate accumulator)
patterns.what: Each food row has a small '+' on the right that adds the default/remembered serving straight to a persistent 'minified Plate' with NO intermediate detail screen. User taps '+' across several rows, then taps 'Log Foods' once to commit the whole meal.
patterns.why: Decouples selection from confirmation. Logging a 5-item breakfast becomes 5 taps + 1 commit instead of 5 full search→detail→save round-trips. Batches the expensive write into a single optimistic commit.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor 'Multi-Add' to minified Plate

patterns.name: Serving-size memory (last-used quantity)
patterns.what: The app stores the exact unit+quantity you last logged for each food and pre-fills it on next add, so '+' logs '1 scoop' or '40g' directly. Favorites can pin a specific preferred serving.
patterns.why: Portion entry is the slowest sub-step of logging. Remembering it removes the numeric-entry step entirely for staples, making the default-serving '+' tap correct ~most of the time.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor (remembers last serving + favorite-with-serving), MyFitnessPal remembered meals

patterns.name: Copy-meal / copy-yesterday / swipe-to-bring-forward
patterns.what: Reuse whole meals across days. MyFitnessPal: '•••' or 'Quick Tools' under a meal → Copy to/from recent or upcoming dates; and a swipe just below the meal name brings forward exactly what was logged under that meal yesterday — no search. 'Remembered Meals' saves a named bundle for one-tap re-logging.
patterns.why: Diets are routine; people eat the same breakfast for weeks. Copy-yesterday converts an entire meal into a single gesture, the highest-leverage shortcut for habitual loggers.
patterns.exampleApp: MyFitnessPal (copy meal, swipe-below-name copy-yesterday, remembered meals), Cronometer custom meals/recipes

patterns.name: Quick Add (calories/macros only, no food)
patterns.what: A 'Quick Add' entry lets users punch in raw calories (and macros for premium) attached to a meal slot without selecting any food item — for restaurant meals or meal kits where only the totals are known.
patterns.why: Provides an escape hatch so 'I don't know the exact food' never blocks logging. Keeps the diary complete and the streak alive instead of forcing an abandoned search.
patterns.exampleApp: MyFitnessPal Quick Add, MacroFactor Quick Add (cal/protein/fat/carb to Plate)

patterns.name: Unified persistent Actions bottom-sheet
patterns.what: A single bottom-sheet 'Actions Sheet' anchors all entry modes (Search, Barcode, Custom Food, Recipe, Quick Add) at the bottom of the screen and persists while the user navigates between them, with a drag-handle line and snap points (peek / half / full).
patterns.why: Bottom sheets keep the diary context visible behind the entry surface, cut cognitive load and render faster than navigating to a new page, and keep the primary action in thumb reach. The visible drag handle signals resizability. NN/g + Material 3 + Apple HIG all converge on this for mobile.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor unified Actions Sheet; pattern endorsed by Material Design 3 / Apple HIG / NN/g 2025-26

patterns.name: Search-as-you-type with debounce + grouped results
patterns.what: Results filter live on each keystroke (debounced ~150-300ms to avoid querying every character), grouped by source with the most relevant 'From History' group first. Results are tappable rows showing name, brand, serving, and kcal/macros inline.
patterns.why: Live feedback removes the explicit 'Search' button tap and lets users stop typing the instant they see the match. Debounce protects DB IO. History-first grouping exploits the repeat-log reality.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor (From History / Custom / Common / Branded groups), MyFitnessPal live search

patterns.name: Portion entry: numeric field + unit picker, 3-6 step max wizard
patterns.what: Serving editor pairs a numeric quantity field (with stepper +/- for fine nudge) and a unit dropdown (g / scoop / cup / piece). Any multi-screen entry stays within a 3-6 step wizard with free backward navigation so users can edit prior inputs without restarting.
patterns.why: Steppers suit small discrete adjustments; a raw numeric field beats a slider for precise gram values (sliders are imprecise for nutrition). 2026 stepper guidance: never block back-nav — editing a copied/pre-filled quantity must be frictionless.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor serving screen; 2026 stepper UX guidance (Lollypop)

patterns.name: Optimistic UI + guaranteed Undo toast
patterns.what: On commit, the food appears in the diary and totals update instantly (optimistic), with a bottom toast 'Logged — Undo' that auto-dismisses ~3s. Undo silently reverts the optimistic state if the write fails or the user changes their mind. Outcome-first copy ('Logged 320 kcal') over generic 'Saved'.
patterns.why: Flips confirm-then-act into act-then-undo: removes a decision point, makes the app feel instant even on slow networks, and the undo net replaces scary 'Are you sure?' dialogs. Aligns with EVA's existing useOptimistic on nutrition meal logs.
patterns.exampleApp: Gmail archive-undo pattern; food apps' diary commit; UX Tigers / NN/g toast guidance

patterns.name: Tap-to-log / swipe-to-action on diary & result rows
patterns.what: Tap a recent/favorite row = log default serving immediately. Swipe-left on a logged diary item reveals destructive/secondary actions (delete, edit, move meal); swipe-right (or threshold drag) can complete/duplicate. Long-press opens quick context menu.
patterns.why: Gestures replace visible buttons, saving screen space and feeling 'addictive/smooth'; swipe-to-reveal keeps the row clean while exposing power actions only on demand.
patterns.exampleApp: MyFitnessPal swipe meal actions, Cronometer tap-to-add favorite; react-swipeable-list / Motion.dev swipe-actions

patterns.name: Empty states that seed the first log
patterns.what: An empty diary/meal slot shows an inviting prompt + the fastest path in (a big 'Add food' CTA, plus 'Copy yesterday' / suggested favorites) rather than a blank void. Onboarding seeds favorites/recents so the pre-search lists aren't empty on day one.
patterns.why: The cold-start problem: surfaced-recents patterns are worthless with no history. A guiding empty state converts the first session and bootstraps the data that makes every later log one tap.
patterns.exampleApp: MyFitnessPal new 'Today' tab home centering logging; Cronometer quick-add menu on empty Diary Group

patterns.name: Haptic confirmation on key actions
patterns.what: Light impact haptic on successful add/commit, a distinct success notification haptic on meal completion / goal hit, and a subtle tick when a swipe crosses its action threshold. Respect a reduced-haptics / system setting and never buzz on every keystroke.
patterns.why: Tactile feedback confirms a tap/swipe 'worked' without the user watching the screen — critical for the heads-down, repetitive act of logging. Personalized/restrained haptics is a 2024-2026 trend; overuse desensitizes.
patterns.exampleApp: expo-haptics / Web Vibration API; Android & Saropa 2025 haptics UX guidance

animations.name: Add-to-plate fly / count-up totals
animations.trigger: Tap '+' on a search/favorite row (multi-add)
animations.spec: ~200-260ms ease-out: row '+' briefly scales (1→1.15→1) while the minified Plate badge count-ups and its macro ring animates to the new value (number tween + arc fill). Library: Framer Motion (web) / Reanimated withTiming. Gives instant 'it landed' feedback without leaving search.
animations.reducedMotion: Skip the fly/scale; snap the count and ring to final value instantly (no tween).

animations.name: Swipe-to-reveal actions + threshold snap
animations.trigger: Horizontal drag on a diary/result row
animations.spec: Row follows finger 1:1; action buttons reveal underneath. On release past ~40% width it springs open to a snap point, else springs back. withSpring (stiffness ~300, damping ~30) or Framer Motion drag with dragConstraints + snap. Fire a light haptic exactly when the threshold is crossed.
animations.reducedMotion: Reduce spring overshoot to a linear 120ms settle; keep the snap but no bounce.

animations.name: Optimistic insert + undo-toast slide
animations.trigger: Commit ('Log Foods')
animations.spec: New diary item slides/fades in from top (~180ms ease-out) with a brief highlight tint that fades over ~1s; simultaneously a toast slides up from bottom-safe-area, holds ~3s, then auto-slides out. Framer Motion AnimatePresence / layout animation. If undo tapped, item collapses (height→0, opacity→0, ~150ms).
animations.reducedMotion: Cross-fade only (no slide/height collapse); toast appears/disappears with opacity, no motion.

animations.name: Bottom-sheet snap transitions
animations.trigger: Open entry sheet / drag handle between snap points
animations.spec: Sheet springs between peek/half/full snap points tracking the drag; backdrop scrim opacity interpolates with sheet height. withSpring (damping ~35) or vaul/Framer Motion sheet. Drag-handle line is the affordance.
animations.reducedMotion: Use a 200ms ease translate instead of spring; scrim fades linearly.

animations.name: Success checkmark on meal complete
animations.trigger: Meal slot fully logged / daily goal reached
animations.spec: Small checkmark draws via SVG path stroke-dashoffset (~300ms) with a single spring pop (scale 0→1.1→1), paired with a success-notification haptic. Framer Motion pathLength or Reanimated. Celebratory but brief — no confetti spam.
animations.reducedMotion: Show the checkmark statically filled (no draw/pop), keep the haptic.

animations.name: Search results live filter (debounced)
animations.trigger: Each keystroke in the search field
animations.spec: Results list re-renders ~150-300ms after last keypress; entering/leaving rows fade+slide ~120ms via AnimatePresence/layout so the list doesn't jump. Stale rows fade out as new ones fade in.
animations.reducedMotion: Instant list swap, no per-row fade/slide.

desktopVsResponsive: On mobile/PWA the entry surface is a thumb-reach bottom sheet with drag-handle snap points, swipe-to-reveal row actions, and haptic confirmation (Web Vibration API); copy-yesterday is a swipe-below-the-meal-name gesture. On desktop the same flows surface as a 'Quick Tools' '•••' dropdown / right-rail panel or modal (no bottom sheet, no swipe, no haptics), copy-meal is an explicit menu item, and hover states replace long-press. Search-as-you-type, favorites/recents/hourly-go-tos pre-lists, multi-add '+', serving memory, and optimistic+undo are identical across both. Key constraint for EVA: h-dvh not h-screen for the sheet, pb-safe on the toast/sheet, and overflow-x:clip; gate haptics behind feature/reduced-motion detection since iOS Safari PWA haptic support is partial.
accessibility: Provide non-gesture equivalents for every swipe (visible edit/delete buttons or a '•••' menu) so logging is operable without gestures. Respect prefers-reduced-motion (degrade all animations above) and any reduced-haptics setting; never rely on haptics or color alone to signal success — pair with a text toast ('Logged 320 kcal'). Toasts must be aria-live=polite and persist long enough (>=3s) plus be re-findable, since auto-dismiss can be missed by screen-reader and low-vision users; the Undo action needs a focusable button, not just the toast tap. Steppers/numeric portion fields need labels, type=number with inputmode=decimal, and large >=44px touch targets. Search-as-you-type results should announce result count via aria-live and keep keyboard focus in the input. Ensure 4.5:1 contrast on macro rings/highlight tints and don't trap focus inside the bottom sheet (Escape / drag-down closes, focus returns to trigger).

area: Responsive + PWA + mobile-first design patterns (2025-2026) for data-dense health/nutrition apps — desktop↔mobile/PWA strategy for the SAME nutrition views

patterns.name: Single breakpoint switch at 768px: bottom-nav (mobile) ↔ persistent sidebar (desktop)
patterns.what: Use one primary mobile/desktop boundary (~768px, Tailwind md:). Below it: fixed bottom tab bar (3-5 primary destinations: Today, Nutrition, Workout, Progress). At/above it: the bottom bar is replaced by a persistent left sidebar/side-rail; the SAME nutrition page reflows from one column into the sidebar+content shell. Avoid a hamburger for primary destinations on mobile — reserve the drawer only for secondary/account items.
patterns.why: Late-2025 research: 75% of phone interactions are one-thumbed; the comfortable zone is the bottom third + the side curve. Primary actions must live where the thumb already is. 768px is the de-facto industry boundary where enough width exists for a persistent rail. Bottom-nav also survives the iOS PWA standalone chrome (no browser tab bar to compete with).
patterns.exampleApp: MyFitnessPal, Cronometer, Lose It! (bottom-tab mobile → sidebar/top-nav web)

patterns.name: Day navigator as a sticky, swipeable header (with tap + arrow fallback)
patterns.what: Pin the date selector to the top (position: sticky; top: env(safe-area-inset-top)). Support horizontal swipe between days AND visible prev/next chevrons + a tappable date that opens a calendar bottom-sheet. On desktop, the same component becomes a wider inline date strip / mini-week selector since width is no longer scarce. Pair each day-swipe with a short slide/fade transition and (on capable devices) haptic feedback.
patterns.why: 2026 gesture guidance: compound gestures need a visible, tap-based fallback or you exclude motor-impaired users (WCAG). Swipe is the natural mental model for day-over-day logging but must never be the ONLY way to change days. Sticky keeps the current day anchored as the meal list scrolls.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor, Cronometer (swipe-between-days diary), Apple Health

patterns.name: Card-stack meals on mobile → multi-column grid on desktop (same data model)
patterns.what: Each meal/log entry is a card (MealCard) stacked single-column on mobile. On desktop, the identical cards flow into a 2-3 column CSS Grid (grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(20rem,1fr))). Never render a horizontally-scrolling data table on mobile — rows become cards where each cell is a labeled key/value. Use container queries (cq) on the card itself so a card in a narrow desktop sidebar still renders its compact 1-column variant.
patterns.why: Data-dense tables don't collapse gracefully; the proven pattern is row→card on narrow widths. Container queries (93%+ support Dec 2025, Safari 16+) let the card adapt to its OWN width, not the viewport — so the same component is correct in the mobile feed, the desktop grid, and a narrow rail without extra breakpoints.
patterns.exampleApp: Cronometer diary, Stripe/Linear dashboards (table→card-stack)

patterns.name: Macro rings/summary as a sticky condensed bar on scroll
patterns.what: The daily macro summary (MacroRingSummary/AdherenceStrip) shows full rings at top of page, then collapses into a thin sticky progress strip (kcal + 3 macro bars) once scrolled past, so remaining targets stay visible while logging. On desktop, the full ring summary stays pinned in the sidebar/right rail instead of collapsing — width permits persistent display.
patterns.why: Users logging meals need the 'remaining today' number constantly visible without scrolling back up; a collapsing sticky header is the standard data-dense solution. Desktop has the spatial budget to keep it fully expanded, so the SAME data renders differently by available space, not by hiding it.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor (collapsing macro header), MyFitnessPal remaining-calories bar

patterns.name: Bottom sheets for secondary flows (food search, exchange equivalences, filters)
patterns.what: Replace center modals with bottom sheets that rise from the thumb zone for: add/search food, exchange equivalences (ExchangeEquivalencesSheet), portion editing, nutrient breakdowns. Drag-to-dismiss + backdrop tap. On desktop the same content renders as a right-side slide-over panel or popover rather than a bottom sheet.
patterns.why: 2026 pattern: bottom sheets replace modal dialogs because they originate in the reachable bottom third and preserve context (the diary stays partially visible behind). The component's content is shared; only the container (sheet vs slide-over) swaps at the breakpoint.
patterns.exampleApp: MacroFactor food search sheet, Notion/Linear slide-overs on desktop

patterns.name: Safe-area + dvh viewport discipline for installed PWA
patterns.what: viewport-fit=cover in the meta viewport; fixed top header uses padding-top: env(safe-area-inset-top), bottom nav uses padding-bottom: env(safe-area-inset-bottom). Use 100dvh / min-h-dvh for full-height shells (never 100vh on mobile — it ignores the dynamic browser/standalone chrome). Set overscroll-behavior-y: contain on scroll containers to kill accidental pull-to-refresh inside the diary; overscroll-behavior: none on the root to stop rubber-band bounce in standalone.
patterns.why: Without viewport-fit=cover, all env(safe-area-inset-*) resolve to 0 and iOS letterboxes the PWA. dvh is the only height unit that accounts for the collapsing URL bar and the standalone Dynamic-Island layout. This matches the repo's existing rule (h-dvh / pl-safe utilities) — extend it to every sticky header and bottom nav in the nutrition views.
patterns.exampleApp: firt.dev PWA power-tips reference; Apple Health / iOS standalone web apps

patterns.name: Custom, deferred, dismissible install prompt (Android) + manual A2HS hint (iOS)
patterns.what: Capture beforeinstallprompt, preventDefault(), stash the event, and surface a custom non-blocking install affordance (snackbar or a small banner like the repo's PushNotificationBanner) only AFTER a value moment — e.g., the user logs their first meal or completes a check-in. Show it only when display-mode: browser. On iOS (no beforeinstallprompt), detect Safari + non-standalone and show a one-time 'Add to Home Screen via Share' hint with the share-icon. Respect a dismiss cooldown; re-prompt only on a later beforeinstallprompt.
patterns.why: Browsers fire the event once and re-firing is gated; prompting cold has low conversion. Deferring to an engaged moment (post-first-log) is the documented high-conversion pattern. iOS Chrome/Edge can't install at all — Safari manual A2HS is the only path, so the hint must be Safari-specific to avoid lying to users.
patterns.exampleApp: web.dev customize-install guidance; Twitter/X PWA deferred install banner

patterns.name: Optimistic logging with offline queue + lightweight sync indicator
patterns.what: On 'log meal', update the UI instantly (useOptimistic, already in the repo's stack) and queue the mutation in IndexedDB. Show a per-item subtle pending state (clock/'syncing' dot) rather than a blocking spinner; clear it on confirm. A global, unobtrusive offline banner appears only when navigator.onLine is false. Use Background Sync where available (Chrome/Edge/Samsung) as progressive enhancement, with a foreground retry-on-reconnect fallback (Safari/Firefox have no Background Sync).
patterns.why: The UI must never wait on the network for a log action — the 2026 offline-first baseline. Per-item optimistic indicators beat full-screen spinners for data-dense logging where users add many entries quickly. Background Sync is not on Safari/iOS, so it can only be an enhancement, never the sole retry mechanism — critical for a PWA whose iOS users are install-via-Safari.
patterns.exampleApp: Cronometer/MyFitnessPal offline diary, Google Keep optimistic sync

desktopVsResponsive: The governing principle for 2025-2026 is ONE component, multiple containers — the same nutrition views (DayNavigator, MealCard, MacroRingSummary, AdherenceStrip, NutritionShell in this repo) reflow by AVAILABLE SPACE, not by hiding data. Concrete desktop-vs-mobile/PWA differences: (1) Navigation: mobile = fixed bottom tab bar in the thumb zone; desktop = persistent left sidebar/side-rail (single switch ~768px / Tailwind md:); the hamburger is mobile-only and for secondary items only. (2) Meal list: mobile = single-column card stack; desktop = 2-3 column auto-fit grid of the SAME cards; container queries (cq) keep each card correct even inside a narrow desktop rail. (3) Macro summary: mobile = full rings at top that COLLAPSE into a thin sticky kcal/macro strip on scroll; desktop = full rings stay PINNED in the sidebar (no collapse — width permits). (4) Day navigation: mobile = sticky swipeable header + calendar bottom-sheet; desktop = wider inline mini-week strip, swipe optional. (5) Secondary flows (food search, exchanges, filters): mobile = bottom sheet rising from thumb zone; desktop = right-side slide-over/popover — shared content, swapped container. (6) PWA-only concerns live almost entirely on mobile/standalone: viewport-fit=cover + env(safe-area-inset-*) on the top header and bottom nav, 100dvh shells, overscroll-behavior containment, the deferred custom install prompt (Android) / Safari A2HS hint (iOS), and optimistic-offline sync indicators. On desktop the browser chrome is stable, so safe-area insets resolve to 0, dvh≈vh, install is a one-click omnibox affordance, and offline is rarer — so the PWA-specific UI gracefully no-ops. Tailwind-mobile-first ordering (base = mobile, md:/lg: = desktop) plus container queries on the data cards is the implementation that makes the SAME view serve all three contexts (mobile web, installed PWA, desktop) without forked components.

animations.name: Day swipe transition
animations.trigger: Horizontal swipe or prev/next chevron on the sticky DayNavigator
animations.spec: Directional slide+fade of the meal-list container, ~200-250ms, ease-out (CSS transition or Framer Motion AnimatePresence with x: ±24px → 0). Pair with a light haptic tick on capable devices. Library: existing CSS transitions or framer-motion if already present.
animations.reducedMotion: Under prefers-reduced-motion: reduce, drop the x-translate and use an instant or sub-100ms crossfade only; date still updates.

animations.name: Macro summary collapse on scroll
animations.trigger: Page scroll past the full ring summary
animations.spec: Full rings shrink/fade into the thin sticky strip over ~150-200ms, ease-in-out, driven by scroll position (IntersectionObserver sentinel, not per-frame scroll handler). Height + opacity interpolation only.
animations.reducedMotion: Snap between expanded and condensed states with no tween; keep the sticky strip functional.

animations.name: Bottom sheet present/dismiss
animations.trigger: Open food search / exchange equivalences; drag-down or backdrop tap to dismiss
animations.spec: Translate-Y from 100% to rest with a spring (stiffness ~300, damping ~30) or ~250ms cubic-bezier ease-out; backdrop fades 0→0.4 opacity. Drag-to-dismiss follows the finger then settles. Library: @base-ui/react / Radix Dialog as a sheet, or framer-motion drag.
animations.reducedMotion: Replace the slide with a short opacity fade (~120ms); disable drag-follow inertia.

animations.name: Optimistic log confirm
animations.trigger: Meal/item logged (useOptimistic) then server confirms
animations.spec: New card mounts instantly with a subtle 'pending' dot; on confirm, a ~180ms scale-in (0.97→1) + dot→check crossfade. Failure: gentle shake (~2px, 1 cycle) + retry affordance.
animations.reducedMotion: No scale or shake; swap pending-dot to check instantly, show inline text status.

animations.name: Install / offline banner entry
animations.trigger: Deferred install moment, or navigator goes offline/online
animations.spec: Slide-down from top (or up from above bottom-nav) ~200ms ease-out; auto-dismiss or manual close. Non-blocking, above safe-area inset.
animations.reducedMotion: Instant show/hide with opacity only.

accessibility: Every gesture needs a visible non-gesture fallback (WCAG 2.5.1 / dragging-movements 2.5.7): day-swipe must keep prev/next chevrons + a tappable date; swipe-to-delete/log a meal must have a tap (overflow menu or button) equivalent — otherwise motor-impaired and screen-reader users are excluded. Respect prefers-reduced-motion on ALL transitions above (slide→crossfade or instant). Maintain ≥44×44px tap targets in the bottom nav and on day chevrons. Bottom sheets and slide-overs must trap focus, be dismissible with Escape, and announce via role=dialog + aria-label. The collapsing macro strip must keep an accessible name and live-region announce 'remaining' updates politely. Ensure dark-mode (repo requirement) and light-mode both meet 4.5:1 contrast for macro numbers and adherence states; don't encode adherence by color alone — pair with icon/label. The install/offline banners must be reachable in DOM/focus order and not auto-steal focus. Honor safe-area insets so fixed controls aren't under the notch/home indicator where they'd be hard to hit.

area: Coach/clinician dashboard UI/UX for reviewing a client's nutrition — desktop-dense, also responsive (2025-2026 patterns from Trainerize, Everfit, Nutrium, Healthie, Practice Better + dense-dashboard and motion research)

patterns.name: Roster compliance table with auto-tagged at-risk flags
patterns.what: The default coach landing view is a scannable list/table of ALL clients, one row each, showing a weekly nutrition-compliance score (% of days the client hit all goals). Clients who fall below a coach-set threshold are auto-tagged 'low compliance' / at-risk so the coach can triage who to follow up with before opening anyone. The score is computed on a fixed weekly cadence (Trainerize recomputes Sunday midnight, client local time).
patterns.why: Coaches manage many clients and cannot deep-read each one. A roster with a single derived KPI per row + an automatic at-risk flag turns 'review everyone' into 'review the flagged few'. This is the dominant triage pattern across Trainerize/Everfit coach views.
patterns.exampleApp: ABC Trainerize (auto client tags, weekly nutrition compliance score), Everfit

patterns.name: Compound daily compliance (all-or-nothing macro day)
patterns.what: A 'compliant day' requires hitting EVERY target (calories + protein + carbs + fat); miss protein alone and the whole day is non-compliant. The weekly score is the count of fully-compliant days. Coach UI surfaces this as a small per-day grid/dots (7 cells = 7 days) so a glance shows the streak and which day broke.
patterns.why: A single honest adherence number prevents 'looks fine on calories but ignored protein' false positives, and the 7-cell day strip gives at-a-glance pattern reading (weekends slipping, mid-week strong) without a chart.
patterns.exampleApp: ABC Trainerize nutrition compliance

patterns.name: KPI strip in the top-left hot zone, inverted-pyramid layout
patterns.what: On the single-client deep-dive, put 3-5 primary numbers (avg calories vs target, protein adherence %, weekly compliance %, weight trend) as a horizontal KPI card strip across the top-left Z-scan path. Each card = big headline number + small delta + tiny sparkline. Secondary detail lives below or in a 'Details' tab.
patterns.why: Eye-tracking Z/F-scan: critical numbers belong on the start of the scan path. Big type for the primary metric, restrained color, supporting stats below = fast comprehension on one laptop screen.
patterns.exampleApp: DataCamp dashboard-design guidance; general analytics KPI strips

patterns.name: Trend card = headline + sparkline + contextual delta vs prior week
patterns.what: Each metric card layers three things: the current value (headline), a small trend visualization (sparkline/mini bar), and a contextual delta comparing to the prior period or the target ('protein 142g, +18g vs last week', up-arrow green). Always pair a KPI with a target OR a prior period OR a benchmark — never a bare number.
patterns.why: A number without comparison is not actionable. The prior-week delta is exactly the 'is this client improving or sliding?' question a coach asks in a weekly review. The sparkline gives direction without a full chart.
patterns.exampleApp: DataCamp pattern; Everfit macro report by day/week/month

patterns.name: Macro report with period toggle (day / week / month)
patterns.what: The nutrition deep-dive offers a single time-grain toggle (day | week | month) that re-scopes all charts and tables at once — calories and macro breakdowns plotted over the chosen window, plus an averages row.
patterns.why: Coaches review at different altitudes: day-level when debugging a bad week, week/month for the check-in narrative. One toggle re-scoping the whole view keeps context stable.
patterns.exampleApp: Everfit (macro report and journal by day, week, or month)

patterns.name: Reserved-meaning color as signal, not decoration
patterns.what: Fix a tiny palette: brand neutrals for chrome, ONE highlight color for 'pay attention', ONE reserved color for risk/alert (red/amber). Status flags and at-risk rows use only the reserved color; on-track uses neutral/green. Avoid rainbow per-metric coloring.
patterns.why: When color has stable meaning, an at-risk row is detectable peripherally across a long roster. Rainbow palettes destroy that signal. This is the core dense-dashboard color rule.
patterns.exampleApp: DataCamp / dense-dashboard guidance

patterns.name: Progressive disclosure: roster summary → client → details tab
patterns.what: Three altitudes. (1) Roster: one KPI + flag per client. (2) Client overview: KPI strip + trend cards on one screen. (3) Details/drill-down tab: per-meal logs, raw entries, photos, notes. Keep the core client view within one laptop screen; push fine detail to a secondary tab or drawer.
patterns.why: Prevents overwhelming the coach while preserving access to raw data. Matches how coaches actually work: scan → assess → investigate only when a flag demands it.
patterns.exampleApp: DataCamp progressive-disclosure; Practice Better / Healthie client portals

patterns.name: Frozen identity column + sortable, sparkline-bearing rows
patterns.what: In the roster table: freeze the client name/avatar column while metric columns scroll horizontally; make columns sortable with a visible directional arrow (sort by lowest compliance to float at-risk to the top); embed a row sparkline for the weekly trend; large click targets to open the client.
patterns.why: Lets a coach sort the whole book by 'worst adherence first' and read each client's trajectory inline without opening them. Frozen identity keeps orientation during horizontal scroll.
patterns.exampleApp: DataCamp operational-table guidance

patterns.name: Weekly check-in review as the organizing unit
patterns.what: The review surface is framed around the week: this-week vs last-week side by side, compliance %, weight delta, and a space for coach notes/feedback tied to that week's data. Engagement + compliance metrics are bundled so the coach sees both 'did they log' and 'did they hit targets' together.
patterns.why: Coaching cadence is weekly; framing the dashboard as a weekly comparison matches the actual workflow (write feedback once a week) and makes 'better or worse than last week' the headline question.
patterns.exampleApp: Trainerize weekly compliance + engagement metrics; Everfit

animations.name: Sparkline / chart draw-on
animations.trigger: Card or chart enters viewport (client opened, or roster row expanded)
animations.spec: 200-400ms line/bar draw-in with ease-out; only the trend path animates, axis/labels render static immediately. Library: Recharts/visx animate prop, or Framer Motion path length. Purpose is to direct attention to the trend, not decorate.
animations.reducedMotion: Under prefers-reduced-motion, render the final chart state instantly (no draw). Essential data must be present with motion disabled.

animations.name: KPI delta count-up
animations.trigger: KPI strip mounts on client deep-dive
animations.spec: Short 300-500ms number count-up to the value, ease-out, with the up/down delta arrow fading in at the end. Keep to the small number of simultaneous changes rule — stagger cards ~40-60ms apart, not all at once.
animations.reducedMotion: Show final number immediately; no count-up.

animations.name: At-risk flag pulse (one-shot)
animations.trigger: Roster loads and a client crosses the at-risk threshold
animations.spec: Single subtle 1x pulse/highlight (~600ms) on newly-flagged rows to draw the eye, then settle to the static reserved-color badge. Never loop — looping motion is distracting in analytics.
animations.reducedMotion: Static reserved-color badge only, no pulse.

animations.name: Period-toggle crossfade
animations.trigger: Coach switches day/week/month grain
animations.spec: 150-250ms crossfade + slight y-shift between the old and new chart data so the view feels continuous rather than flashing. Slow objects down, show few changes at once (per perceptual-capacity guidance).
animations.reducedMotion: Instant swap, no crossfade.

desktopVsResponsive: Desktop-dense is the coach's primary surface: multi-column roster table with horizontal metric columns, KPI strip + 2-3 trend cards per row, side-by-side this-week/last-week comparison, sidebar nav preserving full width for the data. The sidebar-layout pattern is preferred when coaches switch clients and apply filters frequently. On mobile/PWA (coach checking on the go, per Trainerize's mobile coach dashboard): collapse the roster table into stacked client cards (avatar + compliance % + at-risk badge + one sparkline), KPI strip reflows to a 2-up or 1-up scrollable row, comparison stacks vertically (this week above last week) instead of side-by-side, and the details tab becomes a full-screen drill-down rather than an adjacent panel. Keep the at-risk flag and the single compliance number visible in the collapsed card so triage still works one-handed.
accessibility: Never encode at-risk by color alone — pair the reserved color with a text label/badge ('At risk', 'Low compliance') and/or an icon so it survives color-blindness and grayscale. Maintain WCAG contrast for KPI headline numbers and small sparkline strokes (avoid thin low-contrast lines). Honor prefers-reduced-motion: every animation above must degrade to its final static state, and essential adherence data must be fully present with motion off. Roster table needs proper header/row semantics, sortable columns operable by keyboard with announced sort direction, and the frozen identity column must remain reachable. Provide a visible 'Reduce motion' fallback expectation even though it is OS-driven. Give charts text/table equivalents (the averages row and the per-day compliance grid double as the non-visual representation).

area: Accessibility, dark mode, and reduced-motion rules for animated nutrition data visualization (macro rings, calorie/number counters, weekly bar/line charts) — WCAG 2.2 AA baseline, 2025-2026 practice

patterns.name: Macro ring as role=progressbar with value + label fallback
patterns.what: Each macro/calorie ring is a focusable element with role="progressbar", aria-valuenow/aria-valuemin/aria-valuemax (e.g. valuemin=0, valuemax=goal grams, valuenow=consumed) plus aria-valuetext='Proteina: 92 de 140 g (66%)'. If the ring is purely decorative-with-a-number-inside, instead expose role="img" on the SVG with aria-label carrying the same sentence, and mark the inner <svg> arcs aria-hidden. Never leave a bare SVG (invisible to AT).
patterns.why: SVG/canvas arcs are invisible to screen readers unless semantics are added (WCAG 1.1.1, 4.1.2). aria-valuetext gives a human sentence instead of a raw number; progressbar communicates the goal relationship that the visual ring conveys.
patterns.exampleApp: Apple Health activity rings (VoiceOver reads 'Move: 320 of 500 calories, 64%'); MyFitnessPal macro donut

patterns.name: Redundant encoding — never color alone for macros
patterns.what: Distinguish protein/carbs/fat by THREE encodings at once: distinct hue, distinct position/order, AND a text label or icon directly on/next to each segment (e.g. 'P 92g', 'C 180g', 'F 55g'). For over/under-goal status do not signal only by green-vs-red; add an icon (check / up-arrow) or word ('meta' / 'excedido').
patterns.why: WCAG 1.4.1 (Use of Color) forbids color as the sole information carrier. ~8% of men have red-green CVD; a green=good/red=over scheme is the single most common nutrition-app failure.
patterns.exampleApp: Cronometer (labels every macro segment with name+grams); Lose It! goal chips with text status

patterns.name: Colorblind-safe macro palette (Wong/Okabe-Ito) over the cultural protein-red/carb-yellow/fat-blue
patterns.what: Pick the 3-4 macro colors from the Wong palette so they survive deuteranopia/protanopia/tritanopia: blue #0072B2, orange #E69F00, bluish-green #009E73, reddish-purple #CC79A7, vermillion #D55E00. Blue+orange is the single safest 2-color pair. Avoid red+green adjacency and avoid red/orange/brown/green clusters. Run the final palette through a CVD simulator before shipping.
patterns.why: Verified colorblind-safe set distinguishable across all three CVD types; removes the red-green confusion that the common nutrition convention (red protein / green veg) introduces.
patterns.exampleApp: Tableau colorblind-safe palette; Datawrapper default qualitative set

patterns.name: Keyboard + focus traversal of chart data points
patterns.what: Tab moves focus to the chart region (the group), then Arrow keys move between bars/ring segments/days; each data point has tabindex and a visible focus ring (>=3:1 contrast against both fill and background, 2px+ offset). Preserve focus after filter/sort/period-switch instead of dumping to page top. Announce filter results via an aria-live="polite" region ('Mostrando semana del 8 de junio').
patterns.why: WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard), 2.4.7 (Focus Visible), 2.4.11 (Focus Not Obscured, new in 2.2), 4.1.3 (Status Messages). Roving-tabindex grouping avoids 31 tab stops on a 31-day chart.
patterns.exampleApp: Highcharts accessibility module (region + arrow-key data navigation); Apple Health weekly bar chart focus

patterns.name: Data table / text-summary equivalent for every chart
patterns.what: Ship a real <table> (or a toggle 'Ver datos') alongside any non-trivial chart, plus a one-sentence text summary of the trend ('Promedio 1.840 kcal/dia, 12% bajo la meta esta semana'). The table is the machine-readable source of truth for AT and for users who can't parse the visual.
patterns.why: WCAG 1.1.1 long-description requirement for complex images; the summary covers 1.4.1/cognitive load and is the most reliable thing a screen reader can consume.
patterns.exampleApp: GOV.UK / European data viz guide (table-behind-chart pattern); Cronometer's tabular nutrient breakdown

patterns.name: Non-text contrast >=3:1 on every chart element
patterns.what: Bars, ring arcs, lines, the goal/target marker, axis lines and the focus indicator must each hit >=3:1 against ADJACENT colors and the background (WCAG 1.4.11). On-chart numeric labels and legend text hit >=4.5:1 (1.4.3). Don't rely on a 1px hairline gridline (sub-3:1) to separate segments — add gaps or contrasting strokes.
patterns.why: 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast applies to graphical objects required to understand the chart; the most-missed item is thin gridlines and pale 'remaining' ring tracks.
patterns.exampleApp: XD.gov / US data design standards color tokens; Datawrapper auto-contrast enforcement

animations.name: Ring fill (stroke-dashoffset sweep)
animations.trigger: On mount / when a meal is logged and the macro value changes
animations.spec: Animate stroke-dashoffset from full to target over 600-900ms, ease-out (cubic-bezier(0.22,1,0.36,1)) or a soft spring. CSS transition on stroke-dashoffset, or Framer Motion / Motion One for React. Counts as a 'progress indicator' so a short version is allowed even under reduced motion.
animations.reducedMotion: Under prefers-reduced-motion: reduce, set the dashoffset to the final value with no transition (or a <=200ms opacity fade in). Author base styles with NO animation, then add the sweep only inside @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference) — opt-in, fail-safe default.

animations.name: Calorie / macro number counter (count-up)
animations.trigger: On mount and on each value change
animations.spec: Tween the displayed integer from old->new over ~500-800ms (Motion React animate-number / a count-up tween). Pair with tabular-nums / font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums so digits don't reflow and jitter.
animations.reducedMotion: Reduced motion: render the FINAL number immediately, no count-up. Implement by gating the tween on the no-preference query (or matchMedia in JS) and writing the static value otherwise. The number is also the accessible name, so AT never depends on the animation.

animations.name: Weekly bar / line chart entrance
animations.trigger: On chart load or period switch (week/month toggle)
animations.spec: Stagger bars growing from baseline ~40-60ms apart, 300-500ms each, ease-out; line charts draw the path via stroke-dashoffset. Keep total under ~1s so it doesn't block reading.
animations.reducedMotion: Reduced motion: no stagger/grow/draw — render the final chart in place, optionally a single <=200ms cross-fade. Remove any parallax, zoom-on-load, or auto-scrubbing entirely (these are vestibular triggers, not just 'fast').

animations.name: Goal-reached / celebration micro-interaction
animations.trigger: When a macro or calorie goal is hit
animations.spec: Small scale-pulse (1.0->1.06->1.0, ~250ms) or a brief confetti/glow. Keep displacement tiny and non-looping.
animations.reducedMotion: Reduced motion: replace with a static state change — a check icon + color/label change ('Meta alcanzada'), no pulse, no confetti, no infinite spin. Also announce via aria-live="polite" so the achievement isn't motion-only.

accessibility: BASELINE = WCAG 2.2 Level AA. Concrete rules: (1) Color is never the only carrier — every macro segment and every goal/over-budget state carries a text label or icon in addition to hue (SC 1.4.1). (2) Contrast: chart text & numbers >=4.5:1 (1.4.3); bars/arcs/lines/markers/axis/focus-ring >=3:1 vs adjacent colors and background (1.4.11). (3) Each ring = role=\"progressbar\" + aria-valuemin/now/max + aria-valuetext sentence, OR an <svg role=\"img\" aria-label=\"...\"> with arcs aria-hidden — never a bare SVG (1.1.1, 4.1.2). (4) Charts: region focusable, Arrow-key roving-tabindex between data points, focus visible (2.4.7) and not obscured (2.4.11, new in 2.2), focus preserved across sort/filter, filter results announced via aria-live=\"polite\" (4.1.3). (5) Provide a data-table or text-summary equivalent for every non-trivial chart (1.1.1). (6) Touch targets: AA minimum 24x24 CSS px with adequate spacing (SC 2.5.8); target 44x44 (Apple / AAA 2.5.5) or 48x48 (Material) for primary controls like 'log meal', period toggles, and tappable ring/segment hit-areas — the visible ring can be small if the hit-area is padded to >=44px. (7) Text scaling / Dynamic Type: layout must survive 200% zoom and large system font without clipping or horizontal scroll (1.4.4 Resize Text, 1.4.10 Reflow at 320 CSS px) — use rem/dvh, tabular-nums for counters, and let labels wrap; don't bake numbers into fixed-size SVG <text> that can't scale. (8) Motion: respect prefers-reduced-motion via opt-in (animate only under no-preference); reduced = final state instantly, only progress indicators / sub-200ms fades allowed, zero parallax/zoom/loop/confetti (2.3.3 AAA but treat as baseline). (9) Don't convey meaning through animation alone — the final value/state is always present statically and in the accessible name. (10) Test the real palette in a CVD simulator and the rings/charts with NVDA/VoiceOver before ship.
desktopVsResponsive: Desktop/web: full keyboard model matters most — Tab to chart region, Arrow keys across data points, visible 2px focus ring, hover tooltips that ALSO appear on focus (not hover-only, SC 1.4.13); 24px target minimum is usually easy. Mobile / installed PWA: pointer accuracy drops, so bump primary controls (log-meal FAB, week/month toggle, segment tap zones) to 44-48px with >=8px spacing even though AA only mandates 24px; rings often shrink, so keep the numeric label and goal text outside/below the ring rather than only inside the arc, and pad an invisible >=44px hit-area around small segments. iOS PWA must honor Dynamic Type and Android the system font scale — test at the largest setting; use h-dvh not 100vh and tabular-nums so animated counters don't reflow the layout. prefers-reduced-motion is read identically across both (CSS media query / matchMedia), and iOS/Android both expose it from the OS Reduce Motion setting, so a single opt-in implementation covers desktop and PWA.
